1: Haggai prophesied in 520 BCE, the second year of Darius I of Persia — 16 years after the first returnees arrived in Jerusalem. The temple reconstruction had stalled: the returning exiles had built their own houses while the temple remained unfinished (Haggai 1:4). Haggai's four oracles (Haggai 1:1-11; Haggai 2:1-9; Haggai 2:10-19; Haggai 2:20-23) call the community to prioritize the temple and promise divine blessing as a result. His contemporary was Zechariah, whose visions (Haggai 1-8) addressed the same restoration community. Together they provide the theological framework for the post-exilic community's task: rebuilding the worship center through which YHWH's presence among his people would be maintained until the coming of the one greater than the temple.
2: 'Thus says YHWH of hosts: This people says the time has not yet come to rebuild the house of YHWH.' The people's rationalization for not rebuilding is quoted: lo et bo (the time has not yet come). The exact phrase is vague — they do not say the time will never come, only that it has not arrived yet. This is the classic psychology of indefinite deferral: the rebuilding is always a future project, never the present one. YHWH addresses them through Haggai without preamble — the oracle responds directly to the excuse.
3: 'Then the word of YHWH came by the hand of Haggai the prophet.' The formula beyad chaggai hanavi ('by the hand of Haggai the prophet') — the prophet as instrument through whom the divine word travels. This is the standard prophetic commissioning formula, here linking the divine word directly to the historical situation just described. Haggai is identified as hanavi (the prophet) — his prophetic status is clear, though unlike most prophets he is given no genealogy or hometown.
4: 'Is it a time for you yourselves to dwell in your paneled houses, while this house lies in ruins?' The rhetorical contrast: the returnees have rebuilt and paneled their own houses (batim sefunim — houses with cedar paneling, the luxury finish associated with royal architecture, cf. 1 Kings 7:3, 7) while YHWH's house lies in ruins. The paneled house was not a poverty-house; cedar paneling was expensive and associated with kings. Haggai's charge is not that the people built at all, but that they built luxuriously for themselves while neglecting the divine house.
5: 'Now, therefore, thus says YHWH of hosts: Consider your ways.' Simu levaveichem al darkechem — literally 'set your hearts on your ways.' The imperative recurs four times in the book (Haggai 1:5, 7; Haggai 2:15, 18) as a refrain calling for reflective attention to the agricultural failure surrounding them. Haggai is asking the people to trace the cause-and-effect: their neglect of the temple is the cause of their economic distress. The call to consider is diagnostic, not merely moral — he wants them to reason their way to the connection between their priorities and their plight.
6: 'You have sown much, and harvested little. You eat, but you never have enough; you drink, but you never have your fill. You clothe yourselves, but no one is warm. And he who earns wages does so to put them into a bag with holes.' The covenant-curse catalog of agricultural futility: abundant sowing but sparse harvest (Deuteronomy 28:38), eating without satisfaction (Leviticus 26:26; Deuteronomy 28:17), drinking without satisfaction, clothing without warmth, wages that disappear (the bag with holes is a powerful economic metaphor for wage inflation and purchasing-power loss). These are the specific curses of Deuteronomy 28 made visible in the post-exilic economy.
7: 'Thus says YHWH of hosts: Consider your ways.' The second call to consideration (repetition of Haggai 1:5) reinforces the diagnostic challenge. Between the two calls, the description of economic failure (Haggai 1:6) was given; now the proposed solution follows. The repetition frames the agricultural curse between two imperatives: look at what is happening, understand why it is happening, then act on the understanding.
8: 'Go up to the hills and bring wood and build the house, that I may take pleasure in it and that I may be glorified, says YHWH.' The specific instruction: go to the hill country, cut timber, and build. The reason given is double: YHWH's pleasure (ve'irzeh bo, 'I will be pleased with it') and his glorification (ve'ekavdah, 'I will be glorified'). The temple is not primarily for human benefit but for divine honor — though human benefit follows as consequence. The connection between divine pleasure with the temple and agricultural blessing is made explicit in Haggai 1:9-11.
9: 'You looked for much, and behold, it came to little. And when you brought it home, I blew it away. Why? declares YHWH of hosts. Because of my house that lies in ruins, while each of you busies himself with his own house.' YHWH's intervention in the economy: he 'blew away' (vanafahti vo) what they brought home. The harvest that barely survived the field disappeared at home — YHWH's breath scattered it. The explicit causal statement: 'because of my house that lies in ruins, while each of you busies himself with his own house.' The inversion of priorities has inverted the blessing.
10: 'Therefore the heavens above you have withheld the dew, and the earth has withheld its produce.' Dew (tal) in the Mediterranean climate of Judah was not merely pleasant but agriculturally essential — the summer dew compensated for the lack of rain and kept crops alive between the rainy seasons. YHWH controls the dew (Deuteronomy 33:28; Psalms 133:3; Proverbs 3:20; Zechariah 8:12). The withholding of dew is the covenant curse of Deuteronomy 28:24 and Leviticus 26:19 (sky like iron, earth like bronze).
11: 'And I have called for a drought on the land and the hills, on the grain, the new wine, the oil, on what the ground brings forth, on man and beast, and on all their labors.' The drought (chorev) is a divine decree, not a natural accident — YHWH called for it (qarati chorev). The scope of the drought encompasses all the categories of Israelite agricultural prosperity: grain, wine, oil (the standard triad of covenant blessing — Deuteronomy 7:13; Haggai 28:51; Hosea 2:8; Joel 1:10), plus people, animals, and all labor. The totality of the curse mirrors the totality of the neglect.
12: 'Then Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, and Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest, with all the remnant of the people, obeyed the voice of YHWH their God, and the words of Haggai the prophet, as YHWH their God had sent him. And the people feared before YHWH.' The response is immediate and comprehensive: the governor, the high priest, and all the remnant (kol she'erit ha'am) obey and fear. The fear (vayira'u) before YHWH is covenant awe in response to the prophetic word. This is one of the rare instances in the prophetic corpus where the prophet is immediately and wholly obeyed by the entire community — a contrast to the resistance Jeremiah and Isaiah experienced.
13: 'Then Haggai, the messenger of YHWH, spoke to the people with YHWH's message, saying, I am with you, declares YHWH.' Haggai is called malach YHWH (messenger of YHWH) — the same title used of angels. In the context of prophetic literature, it designates him as the authorized bearer of divine speech. The message of reassurance — ani itchem, 'I am with you' — is the divine presence formula of the patriarchal narratives (Genesis 26:24; Haggai 28:15; Haggai 31:3) and the Exodus commission (Exodus 3:12). YHWH's presence accompanies the rebuilding from the first day of commitment.
14: 'And YHWH stirred up the spirit of Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and the spirit of Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest, and the spirit of all the remnant of the people. And they came and worked on the house of YHWH of hosts, their God.' YHWH stirred up (vaya'er YHWH et-ruach) all three groups — the divine initiative precedes and enables the human response. The Spirit-rousing language connects to the Exodus narrative (Exodus 35:21 — the Spirit stirring the people to bring offerings for the tabernacle) and to Ezra 1:1 (YHWH stirring the spirit of Cyrus). The actual work begins 23 days after Haggai's first oracle (1:1 is month 6 day 1; Haggai 1:15 will mark month 6 day 24).
15: 'On the twenty-fourth day of the month, in the sixth month, in the second year of Darius the king.' The precise date of the work's beginning: 21 September 520 BCE — exactly 23 days after the first oracle. The dating formula closes the first oracle unit and opens the second (2:1 begins the next oracle). The 23-day gap between prophetic call and actual commencement is realistic: organizing a community building project — acquiring timber, assigning labor, clearing the site — would require several weeks of preparation.
